content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

April 28, 2006


Mark Ashiku
724 South Dora
Ukiah, CA 95482

RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
      Our File No. I-06-051

Dear Dr. Ashiku:

     This letter is in response to your request regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This letter is limited solely to provisions of the Act, and should not be taken as advice regarding any other areas of the law potentially raised by your letter.² Please note that because your request does not identify a specific governmental decision, we are treating it as a request for informal assistance.³

QUESTION

     Does your personal or business relationship with your brother, who owns real property, which includes a rental house, within an area affected by the city's Hillside Zoning Ordinance, preclude you from participating as a city council member in decisions involving the possible modification of said ordinance?

CONCLUSION

     You have an economic interest in your brother by virtue of the fact that he is a source of income to you. You may participate in city council decisions regarding the Hillside Zoning Ordinance unless it is reasonably foreseeable that such decisions will affect your brother's income by $1,000 or more, or specific circumstances exist regarding

_________________________
     ¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear in Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

     ² This advice is applicable and confers immunity only to the extent that the facts provided to us are correct, and that all of the material facts have been disclosed. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

     ³ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; regulation 18329(c), copy enclosed.)

File No. I-06-051
Page No. 2

a particular decision, its financial effect, and the nature of your brother's real property
which make it reasonably foreseeable that a governmental decision will have a material
financial effect on the real property.

FACTS

You hold the elected position of mayor of the City of Ukiah. The city council is
expected to review and potentially modify the city's Hillside Zoning Ordinance.

Your brother owns two single family dwellings on 7 ½ acres within the area
affected by the Hillside Zoning Ordinance. In our telephone conversation, you advised
us that he resides in one of the dwellings and receives rental income from the other. You
have no financial interest in this real property.

You and your brother are both dentists who operate individual practices but share
office space. You are the sole owner of the office and related equipment. Your brother
rents office space and equipment from you. The rent for the office and equipment is
approximately $100,000 per year. He also pays for his share of materials and supplies
and is responsible for essentially all aspects of operating his dental practice. The dental
office is not located within and is more than 500 feet from the area affected by the
Hillside Ordinance.

You and your brother jointly own (in equal shares) two aircraft with a combined
value of approximately $80,000.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).)
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.

The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether
a public official has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a governmental decision.
(Regulation 18700(b).) The general rule, however, is that a conflict of interest exists
whenever a public official makes a governmental decision which has a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of his or her financial interests.

Step One. Are you a "public official" within the meaning of section 87100?

Section 82048 defines a public official as "every member, officer, employee or
consultant of a state or local government agency." You have correctly identified yourself

File No. I-06-051
Page No. 3

as a public official as a member of the city council of Ukiah, which is a local government
agency.

Step Two. By participating in city council decisions involving modification of
the city's Hillside Zoning Ordinance, would you be making, participating in
making, or influencing a governmental decision?

A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official
"participates in a governmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her
position and without significant substantive or intervening review, the official negotiates,
advises, or makes recommendations to the decision maker regarding the governmental
decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision if, for the purpose of influencing the
decision, the official contacts or appears before any member, officer, employee, or
consultant of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.3.)

By voting as a council member on proposed changes to the Hillside Zoning
Ordinance, you would clearly be making a governmental decision.⁴ If, after completing
the eight-step analysis for a particular governmental decision, you conclude that you have
a conflict of interest with respect to a particular decision, then we would direct your
attention to regulation 18702.5(b).⁵

Step Three. What are your economic interests? What are the possible
sources of a conflict of interest?

The economic interests from which conflicts of interest may arise are described in
section 87103 and regulations 18703-18703.5. There are six kinds of economic interests:

1. A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he

⁴ Moreover, if you advise or make recommendations to other decision making bodies regarding
the Hillside Zoning Ordinance, you would be participating in that governmental decision. Finally, any
advice you give or recommendations you make to the decision making body regarding the Hillside Zoning
Ordinance would constitute an attempt to use your official position to influence a governmental decision.

⁵ The regulation provides that if a public official who holds an office specified in section 87200
has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, he or she must: (1) immediately prior to
the discussion of the item, verbally identify each type of economic interest involved in the decision as well
as details of the economic interest on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave
the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed sessions, consent calendars,
absences and speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests, special rules found in
regulation 18702.5(c) and (d) apply. As mayor, you are a public official specified in section 87200.

File No. I-06-051
Page No. 4

or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a);
regulation 18703.1(a).)

2. A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or
she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management.
(Section 87103(d); regulation 18703.1(b).)

3. A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she
has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b); regulation
18703.2.)

4. An official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, totaling $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section
87103(c); regulation 18703.3.)

5. A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her
if the gifts total $360 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e);
regulation 18703.4.)

6. A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family. This is
commonly referred to as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103; regulation
18703.5.)

You clearly have an economic interest in your dental practice as a business entity
in which you have presumably invested $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a); regulation
18703.1(a).)

You also have an economic interest in your brother's dental practice because the
dental practice is a source of income to you by virtue of the rent you receive. Your
brother's dental practice falls within the definition of "business entity" found in section
82005 as "any organization or enterprise operated for profit, including but not limited to a
proprietorship, partnership, firm, business trust, joint venture, syndicate, corporation or
association." Under section 87103(c) and regulation 18703.3 the practice is a source of
income to you as long as you are paid $500 or more in rental payments within the 12-
month period preceding any governmental decision.

You indicate that your brother is the sole owner of his dental practice and that he
is the person who controls the business. We must, therefore, conclude that he himself is a
source of income to you. The Commission has long advised that when the ownership and
control of a business entity is vested in a single person, that person himself will be
regarded as a "source of income" under the Act in addition to the business entity he owns
and operates. (See generally In re Nord, (1983) 8 FPPC Ops. 6, and the Korb Advice
Letter, No. A-99-113.)

File No. I-06-051
Page No. 5

Finally, a public official always has an economic interest in his or her personal
expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family.

Step Four. Will your economic interests be directly or indirectly involved in
decisions you will make or participate in as a city council member?

Regulation 18704.1 provides that "a person, including business entities, sources
of income, and sources of gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an official's
agency when that person, either directly or by an agent: (1) Initiates the proceeding in
which the decision will be made by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar
request; or (2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of the proceeding concerning the
decision before the official or the official's agency." Since it does not appear that you,
your dental practice, your brother's dental practice or your brother will be initiating
proceedings regarding the Hillside Zoning Ordinance nor will they be a party in or the
subject of such proceedings, the economic interests that you have disclosed to us will be
deemed to be indirectly involved in any decisions. (Regulation 18704.1(b).)

As for your economic interest in your personal finances, if facts suggest any
financial effect on your personal finances, your economic interest in your personal
finances is deemed to be directly involved in the governmental decision.

Step Five. Applicable Materiality Standard

Sources of Income

Regulation 18705.3 – 18705.5 sets forth the materiality standards to be applied in
different situations. Regulation 18705.3 differentiates between economic interests in
sources of income which are business entities and those who are individuals.

Since your brother's dental practice is a business entity which is a source of
income to you, we apply the materiality standard set forth in regulation 18705.1(c), as
dictated by regulation 18705.3(b)(1). This is also the standard used to determine
materiality of a decision affecting your dental practice. The materiality threshold
depends on whether the business is a Fortune 500 company, is listed on the New York
Stock Exchange, the American Stock Exchange or the NASDAQ or is qualified for
listing by virtue of its before-tax earnings.

We assume that your and your brother's practices are not listed and so we focus
on their earnings. As to each practice, if in its most recent fiscal year the practice
enjoyed pre-tax earnings of $2.5 million or more, a decision by the city council would
have a material effect only if it results in an increase or decrease to the practice's gross
revenues of $500,000 in a fiscal year; or would result in the practice incurring or avoiding
additional expense or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the

File No. 1-06-051
Page No. 6

amount of $200,000 or more; or would result in an increase or decrease in the value of
the practice's assets or liabilities of $500,000 or more. (Regulation 18705.1(c)(2).)

If in its most recent fiscal year the practice had net income of no less than
$500,000 or before-tax earnings of no less than $750,000, a decision by the city council
would have a material effect only if it results in an increase/decrease of gross revenues of
$300,000 or more; or would result in expenses incurred/avoided/reduced/eliminated of
$100,000 or more; or would result in an increase/decrease in the value of the practice of
$300,000 or more. (Regulation 18705.1(c)(3).)

If none of the above income levels apply, then the financial effect of a
governmental decision on the practice would be material only if it is reasonably
foreseeable that it will result in an increase/decrease of gross revenues of $20,000 or
more; or would result in expenses incurred/avoided/reduced/eliminated of $5,000 or
more; or would result in an increase/decrease in the value of the practice of $20,000 or
more. (Regulation 18705.1(c)(4).)

Since your brother himself is also a source of income to you, we next examine the
financial effect of a city council decision upon him. Having determined that the effect of
a city council decision upon your brother would be indirect, we look to regulation
18705.3(b)(3) which sets forth the materiality standards for decisions that indirectly
affect a source of income who is an individual.

"The effect of a decision is material as to an individual who
is a source of income to an official if any of the following
applies:

(A) The decision will affect the individual's income,
investments, or other tangible or intangible assets or
liabilities (other than real property) by $1,000 or more; or

(B) The decision will affect the individual's real property
interest in a manner that is considered material under
Title 2, California Code of Regulations, sections
18705.2(b)." (Regulation 18705.3(b)(3).)

We apply the standards of regulation 18705.3(b)(3) to analyze decisions that may
affect the following income, investments and assets of your brother: (1) income from his
rental property; (2) investment in and the asset consisting of his real property, the seven
acres of land, including the two single family dwellings; and (3) investment in and the
assets consisting of the two aircraft that he owns jointly with you.

Under regulation 18705.3(b)(A) a governmental decision is material if it will
affect your brother's rental income by $1,000. Similarly, a governmental decision is

File No. 1-06-051
Page No. 7

material if it will affect his investments or assets (other than real property), including the
aircraft, by $1,000. Finally, regulation 18705.3(b)(B) directs us to regulation 18705.2(b)
for the materiality standard for decisions affecting your brother's real property: "The
financial effect of a governmental decision on real property which is directly involved
in the governmental decision is presumed not to be material." (Emphasis added.) Please
note, however, that the presumption may be rebutted by proof that there are specific
circumstances regarding the governmental decision, its financial effect, and the nature of
the real property which make it reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect on the real property.6

Personal Finances

While your aircraft are the only assets that you mention in your letter that appear
to be personal assets, you should analyze each governmental decision in terms of
regulation 18705.5, which provides in pertinent part:

"(a) A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a pubic official's
personal finances is material if it is at least $250 in any 12-month
period."

Step Six. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of a governmental
decision on your economic interest will meet the applicable materiality standard?
(Does a conflict of interest exist?)

Under regulation 18706, an effect upon economic interests is considered
reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur.

Sources of Income

From the facts you have provided, it does not appear that there is a substantial
likelihood that city council decisions involving the Hillside Zoning Ordinance will have a
material financial effect upon your or your brother's dental practices.

However, you must determine if it is reasonably foreseeable that: (1) a particular
governmental decision will affect your brother's income from his rental property by
$1,000; (2) a particular governmental decision will affect his other investments or assets
(other than real property) by $1,000; or (3) there are specific circumstances regarding the
governmental decision, its financial effect, or the nature of the real property that will
negate the presumption that the effect on his real property is not material. If your answer

6 For examples of specific circumstance, see regulation 18705.2(b)(1)(A)-(C) which includes
decisions that will affect the development potential or income producing potential of the real property, the
use of the real property, and the character of the neighborhood, including substantial effects on traffic,
view, privacy, intensity of use, noise levels, air emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood.

File No. 1-06-051
Page No. 8

is "yes" to any of the foregoing questions, you may not participate in that particular
decision."

Personal Finances

You have not advised us of any circumstances suggesting that you will be making
decisions that may affect your personal finances to a material degree.

Ultimately, whether a material financial effect is foreseeable at the time a decision is
made depends on facts and circumstances peculiar to each case. (In re Thorner, (1975)
1 FPPC Ops. 198.) The foreseeability of a particular financial effect is a determination
that must be left, in most instances, to the informed judgment of the public official.

Steps Seven & Eight. Does the "public generally" exception or the "legally
required participation" exception apply?

Even if a material financial effect on a public official's economic interest is
reasonably foreseeable, he or she still may not be disqualified if the financial effect of the
governmental decision on the public official's economic interest is indistinguishable from
the effect on the public generally (section 87103; regulations 18700(b) (7) and 18707(a))
or the official is legally required to participate (section 87103; regulation 18708). You
have not presented any facts indicating that either of these exceptions is applicable in this
instance.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Valentina Joyce
Counsel, Legal Division

7 For example, it may be reasonably foreseeable that certain decisions to modify the Hillside
Zoning Ordinance would affect the developmental potential of your brother's seven acres such that the
decision would have a material impact on the property and the presumption of non-materiality would be
rebutted.

their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).)
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or

the official has a financial interest.
The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether
a public official has a disqualifuing conflict of interest in a govemmental decision.
(Regulation 18700(b).) The general rule, however, is that a conflict of interest exists
whenever a public official makes a goveflrmental decision which has a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of his or her financial interests.
Step One. Are you a oopublic official" within the meaning of section 87100?
Section 82048 defines a public official as "every member, officer, employee or
consultant of a state or local govefirment agency." You have correctly identified yourself
as a public official as a member of the city council of Ukiah, which is a local government
agency.

the city's Hillside zoningordinance, would you be making, participating in
making, or influencing a governmental decision?
A public official "makes a govemmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.L) A public official
"participates in a governmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her
position and without significant substantive or intervening review, the official negotiates,
advises, or makes recommendations to the decision maker regarding the governmental
decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her official
position to influence a goveflrmental decision if, for the purpose of influencing the
decision, the official contacts or appears before any member, officer, employee, or
consultant of his or her ageracy. (Regulation 18702.3.)
By voting as a council member on proposed changes to the Hillside Zoning

The economic interests from which conflicts of interest may arise are described in

1. A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he

or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a);
regulation 1 8703. 1 (a).)
2. A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or
she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management.
(Section 871 03(d); regulation 1 8703. I (b).)
3. A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she
has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section s7103(b); regulation
18703.2.)
4. An official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, totaling $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section
871 03(c); regulation I 8703.3.)
5. A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her
if the gifts total $360 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e);
regulation 18703.4.)
6. A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family. This is
commonly referred to as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103; regulation
18703.s.)
You clearly have an economic interest in your dental practice as a business entity
in which you have presumably invested $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a); regulation
18703.1(a).)
You also have an economic interest in your brother's dental practice because the
dental practice is a source of income to you by virtue of the rent you receive. Your
brother's dental practice falls within the definition of "business entity''found in section
82005 as "any orgarization or enterprise operated for profit, including but not limited to a
proprietorship, partnership, firm, business trust, joint venture, syndicate, corporation or
association." LJnder section 87103(c) and regulation 18703.3 the practice is a source of
income to you as long as you are paid $500 or more in rental payments within the 12-
month period preceding any govemmental decision.
You indicate that your brother is the sole owner of his dental practice and that he
is the person who controls the business. We must, therefore, conclude that he himself is a
source of income to you. The Commission has long advised that when the ownership and
control of a business entity is vested in a single person, that person himself will be
regarded as a "source of income" under the Act in addition to the business entity he owns
and operates. (See generally In re Nord, (19s3) 8 FPPC ops. 6, and the Korb Advice
Letter, No. A-99-113.)
Finally, a public official always has an economic interest in his or her personal
expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family.
Step Four. Wiil your economic interests be directly or indirectly involved in
decisions you will make or participate in as a city council member?
Regulation 18704.1provides that"aperson, including business entities, sources
of income, and sources of gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an official's
agency when that person, either directly or by an agent: (1) Initiates the proceeding in
which the decision will be made by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar
request; or (2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of the proceeding concerning the
decision before the official or the official's agency." Since it does not appear that you,
your dental practice, your brother's dental practice or your brother will be initiating
proceedings regarding the Hillside ZoningOrdinance nor will they be aparty in or the
subject of such proceedings, the economic interests that you have disclosed to us will be
deerned to be indirectly involved in any decisions. (Regulation 18704.1(b).)
As for your economic interest in your personal finances, if facts suggest any
{inancial effect on your personal finances, your economic interest in your personal
finances is deemed to be directly involved in the governmental decision.
Step Five. Applicable Materiality Standard
Sources of Income
Regulation 18705.3 - 18705.5 sets forth the materiality standards to be applied in
different situations. Regulation 18705.3 differentiates between economic interests in
sources of income which are business entities and those who are individuals.
Since your brother's dental practice is a business entity which is a source of
income to you, we apply the materiality standard set forth in regulation 18705. 1(c), as
dictated by regulation 18705.3(b)(1). This is also the standard used to determine
materiality of a decision affecting your dental practice. The materiality threshold
depends on whether the business is a Fortune 500 company, is listed on the New york
Stock Exchange, the American Stock Exchange or the NASDAQ or is qualified for
listing by virtue of its before-tax earnings.
We assume that your and your brother's practices are not listed and so we focus
on their earnings. As to each practice, if in its most recent fiscal year the practice
enjoyed pre-tax earnings of $2.5 million or more, a decision by the city council would
have a material effect only if it results in an increase or decrease to the practice's gross
revenues of $500,000 in a fiscal year; or would result in the practice incurring or avoiding
additional expense or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the
amount of $200,000 or more; or would result in an increase or decrease in the value of
the practice's assets or liabilities of $500,000 or more. (Regulation I 8705.1(cXZ).)
If in its most recent fiscal year the practice had net income of no less than
$500,000 or before-tax earnings of no less than $750,000, a decision by the city council
would have a material effect only if it results in an increase/decrease of gross revenues of
$300,000 or more; or would result in expenses incurred/avoided/reduced/eliminated of
$100,000 or more; or would result in an increase/decrease in the value of the practice of
$300,000 or more. (Regulation 18705. 1(c)(3).)
If none of the above income levels apply, then the financial effect of a
governmental decision on the practice would be material only if it is reasonably
foreseeable thatit will result in an increase/decrease of gross revenues of $20,000 or
more; or would result in expenses incurred/avoided/reduced/eliminated of $5,000 or
more; or would result in an increase/decrease in the value of the practice of $20,000 or
more. (Regulation 18705.1(cX4).)
Since your brother himself is also a source of income to you, we next examine the
financial effect of a city council decision upon him. Having determined that the effect of
a city council decision upon your brother would be indirect, we look to regulation
18705.3(b)(3) which sets forth the materiality standards for decisions that indirectly
affect a source of income who is an individual.
"The effect of a decision is material as to an individual who
is a source of income to an official if any of the following
applies:
(A) The decision will affect the individual's income,
investments, or other tangible or intangible assets or
liabilities (other than real property) by $1,000 or more; or
(B) The decision will affect the individual's real property
interest in a manner that is considered material under
Title 2. California Code of Regulations, sections
187 05.2b)." (Regulation 1 8705.3(bX3).)
We apply the standards of regulation 18705.3(b)(3) to analyze decisions thatmay
affect the following income, investments and assets of your brother: (1) income from his
rental property; (2) investment in and the asset consisting of his real property, the seven
acres of land, including the two single family dwellings; and (3) investment in and the
assets consisting of the two aircraft that he owns jointly with you.
Under regulation 18705.3(b)(A) a governmental decision is material if it will
affect your brother's rental income by $1,000. Similarly, a governmental decision is
mateial if it will affect his investments or assets (other than real property), including the
aircraft, by $1,000. Finally, regulation 18705.3(b)(B) directs us to regulation 18705.2(b)
for the matenality standard for decisions affecting your brother's real property: "The
financial effect of a governmental decision on real property which is indirectly involved
in the governmental decision is presumed not to be material." (Emphasis added.) Please
note, however, that the presumption may be rebutted by poof that there are specific
circumstances regarding the governmental decision, its financial effect, and the nature of
the real property which make it reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect on the real property.6
Personal Finances
While your aircraft are the only assets that you mention in your letter that appear
to be personal assets, you should analyze each governmental decision in terms of
regulation 18705.5, which provides in pertinent part:
"(a) A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a pubic official's
personal finances is material if it is at least $250 in any l2-month
period."
Step Six. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of a governmental
decision on your economic interest will meet the applicable materiality standard?
@oes a conflict of interest exist?)
Under regulation 18706, an effect upon economic interests is considered
reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur.
Sources of Income
From the facts you have provided, it does not appear that there is a substantial
likelihood that city council decisions involving the Hillside ZoningOrdinance will have a
material financial effect upon your or your brother's dental practices.
However, you must determine if it is reasonably foreseeable that: (1) a particular
goveflrmental decision will affect your brother's income from his rental property by
$1,000; (2) aparticular governmental decision will affect his other investments or assets
(other than real property) by $1,000; or (3) there are specific circumstances regarding the
govefitmental decision, its financial effect, or the nature of the real property that will
negate the presumption that the effect on his real property is not material. If your answer
6 For e*amples of specific circumstance, see regulation 18705.2(bXlXA)-(C) which includes
decisions that will affect the development potential or income producing potential of the real property, the
use ofthe real property, and the character ofthe neighborhood, including substantial effects on traffic,
view, privacy, intensity of use, noise levels, air emissions, or similar kaits of the neighborhood.
is "yes" tg *y of the foregoing questions, you may not participate in that particular
decision.T
Personal Finances
You have not advised us of any circumstances suggesting that you will be making
decisions thatmay affect your personal finances to a material degree.
Ultimately, whether amateial financial effect is foreseeable at the time a decision
is made depends on facts and circumstances peculiar to each case. (In re Thorner, (1975)
1 FPPC Ops. 198.) The foreseeability of a particular frnancial effect is a determination
that must be left, in most instances, to the informed judgrnent of the public official.
Steps Seven & Eieht. Does the "public generally" exception or the.6legally
required participation" exception apply?
Even if a material financial effect on a public official's economic interest is
reasonably foreseeable, he or she still may not be disqualified if the financial effect of the
governmental decision on the public official's economic interest is indistinguishable from
the effect on the public generally (section 87103; regulations 187000) (7) and 18707(a))
or the official is legally required to participate (section 87103; regulation 18708). you
have not presented any facts indicating that either of these exceptions is applicable in this
instance.

QUESTION: Does your personal or business relationship with your brother, who owns real
property, which includes arental house, within an area affected by the city's Hillside
ZoningOrdinance, preciude you from participating as a city council member in decisions
involving the possible modification of said ordinance?

CONCLUSION: You have an economic interest in your brother by virtue of the fact that he is a
source of income to you. You may participate in city council decisions regarding the
Hillside Zoning Ordinance unless it is reasonably foreseeable that such decisions will
affect your brother's income by $1,000 or more, or specific circumstances exist regarding

embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 April 28, 2006 Mark Ashiku 724 South Dora Ukiah, CA 95482 RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-06-051 Dear Dr. Ashiku: This letter is in response to your request regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This letter is limited solely to provisions of the Act, and should not be taken as advice regarding any other areas of the law potentially raised by your letter.² Please note that because your request does not identify a specific governmental decision, we are treating it as a request for informal assistance.³ QUESTION Does your personal or business relationship with your brother, who owns real property, which includes a rental house, within an area affected by the city's Hillside Zoning Ordinance, preclude you from participating as a city council member in decisions involving the possible modification of said ordinance? CONCLUSION You have an economic interest in your brother by virtue of the fact that he is a source of income to you. You may participate in city council decisions regarding the Hillside Zoning Ordinance unless it is reasonably foreseeable that such decisions will affect your brother's income by $1,000 or more, or specific circumstances exist regarding _________________________ ¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear in Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. ² This advice is applicable and confers immunity only to the extent that the facts provided to us are correct, and that all of the material facts have been disclosed. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) ³ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; regulation 18329(c), copy enclosed.) File No. I-06-051 Page No. 2 a particular decision, its financial effect, and the nature of your brother's real property which make it reasonably foreseeable that a governmental decision will have a material financial effect on the real property. FACTS You hold the elected position of mayor of the City of Ukiah. The city council is expected to review and potentially modify the city's Hillside Zoning Ordinance. Your brother owns two single family dwellings on 7 ½ acres within the area affected by the Hillside Zoning Ordinance. In our telephone conversation, you advised us that he resides in one of the dwellings and receives rental income from the other. You have no financial interest in this real property. You and your brother are both dentists who operate individual practices but share office space. You are the sole owner of the office and related equipment. Your brother rents office space and equipment from you. The rent for the office and equipment is approximately $100,000 per year. He also pays for his share of materials and supplies and is responsible for essentially all aspects of operating his dental practice. The dental office is not located within and is
